excerpt: The Absolute Truth and its expression through the Qur'anic discourse
source_location_hint: Sermon on Divine Reality and monotheism

excerpt: Knowledge of the Absolute Truth and monotheism is the purpose of all action, whether worship or warfare
source_location_hint: Discourse at the Battle of Jamal

excerpt: The three battles of Jamal, Siffin, and Nahawan were fought in response to the question of divine monotheism
source_location_hint: Historical account of the battles

excerpt: من عرف نفسہ فقد عرف ربہ
source_location_hint: Hadith literature; attributed to the Prophet in multiple hadith collections

excerpt: The meaning of the word أحسان in Arabic is often taken for virtue or excellence. But it also has a meaning حُسن (beauty), that is, such concepts make the soul beautiful.
source_work: Various Islamic philosophical and theological works
source_location_hint: Islamic philosophical tradition on iḥsān and aesthetic virtue

excerpt: Allah is beautiful and loves beautiful things
source_location_hint: Hadith collections; transmitted by ʿĀʾisha and others

excerpt: Death is as dear to me as a mother's breast is to a newborn child
source_author: Amir al-Mu'minīn ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Sermon or statement preserved in Nahj al-Balāghah

excerpt: کان یعظمہ فی عینی، صغار الدنیا فی عینہ
source_author: Amir al-Mu'minīn ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Statements of Amir al-Mu'minīn on asceticism and virtue

excerpt: لو کشف الغطاء ما الزدت یقینا
source_work: Nahj al-Balāghah; Islamic sources on certainty
source_author: Amir al-Mu'minīn ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Statements on utter certainty of faith and knowledge of Allah

excerpt: It is to worship Allah as if you see Him with your own eyes; and if you cannot achieve this, then [worship Him in the awareness] that surely He sees you
source_location_hint: Hadith of Gabriel (Jibrīl) on Islam, Īmān, and Iḥsān; transmitted by ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb

excerpt: Thus the oppressor inflicts upon himself not only the Creator's just wrath through his cruelty to creatures, but also opposes the Creator through his very actions.
source_author: ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib (compiled by al-Sharif al-Radi)
source_location_hint: Letter 53 (al-Katāʾib)

excerpt: When Allah commanded him to prostrate before Adam, Iblīs refused outright, filled with immense pride. He deemed himself superior to Adam, saying, 'I am created of fire, and man is created of clay.'
source_authority: Sermon on Divine Attributes and Satan's Fall
source_location_hint: Sermon 192 (al-Qāṣiyya)

excerpt: A sin that grieves you is better than a good deed that makes you arrogant. Times of praise and glory provide Satan his best opportunities for assault.
source_location_hint: Aphorism 233

excerpt: Because to act for the praise of others rather than for the sake of Allah is to associate partners with Him. Whoever acts for other than Allah will be entrusted by Allah to those others.
source_location_hint: Saying 192

excerpt: Do not say, 'I have the authority, I command and I am obeyed.' Such a notion creates chaos in the heart and ruins religion itself.
source_location_hint: Letter 53

excerpt: Arrogance is a severe form of doubt that springs from a disordered intellect. It concerns itself not only with inflating one's own estimation but also with belittling Truth.
source_authority: Teachings on the Nature of Pride
source_location_hint: Letter 53

excerpt: Do not say, 'I command, and obey me,' for such is the way of that commanding self whose orders the body, soul, imagination, intellect, intention, emotions, and consciousness all obey.
source_location_hint: Letter 53

excerpt: Indeed We have given you abundance, so perform the prayer to your Lord and sacrifice.
source_location_hint: Chapter al-Kawthar

excerpt: There is a group that worships Allah out of desire for Paradise—this is commercial worship. Another group worships from fear of Hell—this is the worship of the slave. And there is a group that worships Allah out of gratitude—this is the worship of the free.
source_work: attributed to ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib via Nahj al-Balagha
source_location_hint: Sermon tradition in Nahj al-Balagha

excerpt: And they feed the needy, and the orphan, and the captive, out of love for Him. Whatever We feed you, it is only for the sake of Allah; it is not for us to take any retribution from you, nor to be grateful.
source_location_hint: Chapter Dahr (or al-Insan)

excerpt: When he ascended to the apparent caliphate, successive storms of war and rebellion arose, so that he could not implement all the principles and strategies he desired. Yet despite these trials, he never faltered, never grew disheartened.
source_work: Sirat and biographical traditions of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Reign of ʿAlī as Caliph (35-40 AH)

excerpt: My servant does not draw near to Me through anything more beloved to Me than the obligatory duties. My servant continues to draw near to Me through supererogatory acts until I love him. When I love him, I am his hearing by which he hears, his sight by which he sees, his hand by which he grasps, and his feet by which he walks.
source_work: Hadith tradition; attributed to Prophet Muhammad
source_location_hint: Hadith on nearness through supererogatory deeds

excerpt: Verily, Allah has made remembrance a means for hearts, through which they hear when deaf and see when blind.
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha or attributed sermon tradition
source_authority: ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib via al-Sharif al-Radi
source_location_hint: Sermon on dhikr and remembrance

excerpt: Junaid said that 'Ali is our master in the knowledge of principles
source_location_hint: discussion of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib and spiritual knowledge

excerpt: Allah has designated the Qur'an as dhikr
source_location_hint: Qur'anic references to Qur'an as dhikr and protection

excerpt: For the Sufis and the saints, dhikr is a systematic path through which the heart absorbs what the intellect comprehends, transforming abstract forms into concrete spiritual realities
source_work: Rahat al-ʿAql and Sufi methodological texts
source_location_hint: discussion of dhikr practice and heart-intellect integration

excerpt: Among them is a chosen group who prefer the remembrance of Allah above all else in this world, living as though they have crossed into the Hereafter while present in this world
source_location_hint: sermon context of remembrance and spiritual life

excerpt: dafāʾin al-ʿuqūl, the buried treasures of intellect, the hidden consciousness within
source_location_hint: description of prophetic function in awakening intellect

excerpt: For everything there is a polish, and the polish of the heart is the remembrance of Allah
source_work: Sunan al-Tirmidhi and other hadith collections
source_location_hint: prophetic sayings on dhikr and heart purification

excerpt: There are angels of Allah who traverse the streets in search of the people of remembrance; and when they encounter them, they say to one another, Come to that which your hearts desire
source_location_hint: hadith on virtues of remembrance and angels

excerpt: My servant ceases not to draw near unto Me through supererogatory works until I love him. And when I love him, I become his hearing by which he hears, his sight by which he sees, his hand by which he grasps, and his feet by which he walks
source_work: Sahih al-Bukhari and other hadith collections
source_location_hint: sacred hadith (hadith qudsi) on divine love and human faculties

excerpt: Allah is in all things, yet not through proximity; and He is apart from all things, yet not through separation
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha, sermons and sayings of ʿAlī
source_location_hint: discussion of divine transcendence and immanence

excerpt: Allah sent forth the Messengers to exact the covenant of His creation, to remind them of blessings forgotten, to awaken the buried treasures of intellect, and to show the signs of His power
source_location_hint: sermon on prophetic function and human primordial knowledge

excerpt: Praise be to Allah, who is known without seeing
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha, opening of sermons and prayers
source_location_hint: doxological and theological opening formulas

excerpt: Were the veil lifted, my certainty would not increase
source_location_hint: sayings on certainty and knowledge of the Divine

excerpt: How did you come to know Allah? By His joining of opposites together: He is the First and the Last, the Outward and the Inward
source_work: Sufi spiritual biographies and Rahat al-ʿAql
source_author: Abū Saʿīd al-Kharrāz / Kirmani exegetical tradition
source_location_hint: epistemology through divine attributes and paradox

excerpt: The Prophet's nocturnal devotion and divine permission for moderation in remembrance, yet the principle remains unshaken
source_location_hint: Surah al-Muzammil, verse 20

excerpt: He who loves a thing speaks of it with passion (man aḥabba shayʾan lahija bidhikrih)
source_authority: Imam and Commander of the Faithful
source_location_hint: Sermon on the virtues of remembrance

excerpt: O Thou whose Name is medicine and whose remembrance is healing (yā man ismuhu dawāʾun wa dhikruhu shifāʾun)
source_location_hint: Invocations of the Prophet

excerpt: Let your tongue always be moist with the remembrance of God—teaching given to a man overwhelmed by Islamic obligations
source_location_hint: Reported regarding ease in religious practice

excerpt: remembrance...differs from monotheism (oneness with another)...process of self-integration
source_location_hint: esoteric metaphysics of remembrance and tawhid

excerpt: negligence removed from dhikr makes it perfect...person forgets oneself
source_location_hint: on the condition of remembrance and self-annihilation

excerpt: harmony between heart and tongue...what is concealed consistent with what is shown...remembrance burns hearts
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha; al-Masabih fi Ithbat al-Imama
source_location_hint: sermons on sincerity and perfection of dhikr

excerpt: My servant approaches through obligatory duties...ceases not through supererogatory acts until I love him

source_location_hint: on the degrees of approach to Allah through remembrance

excerpt: states of perfect monotheism that neither angel nor prophet can bear

source_location_hint: on the supreme states of tawhid

excerpt: Reality is the name denoting manifestation of Allah's glory unmediated by veil
source_location_hint: on haqiqa and unveiling of divine truth

excerpt: more one is destroyed in Allah's name, more one errs in knowledge of the Original

excerpt: We are the Most Beautiful Names of Allah...the Greatest Sign and His Greatest Verses
source_location_hint: on the imams as manifestations of divine attributes

excerpt: perfection of sincerity is to purify from all attributes...every attribute witnesses to its attributer
source_location_hint: on the doctrine of pure monotheism

excerpt: Intellect is that which is capable of a direct contemplative vision of transcendent realities. Reason, on the other hand, is of an indirect, discursive nature.
source_location_hint: metaphysical epistemology

excerpt: Ali will fight for the taweel of the Quran as I fought for its tanzil.
source_work: Various hadith compilations and Nahj al-Balagha commentary traditions
source_author: Prophet Muhammad (PBUH) - transmitted in Ismaili sources
source_location_hint: hadith literature and sermon interpretation

excerpt: What the bedouin wishes is what we wish for the people—knowledge of divine oneness at the heart of all action.
source_authority: Primary source on Imam Ali's teachings
source_location_hint: Sermon 1 and related discourses on tawḥīd

excerpt: That which has no second does not enter into the category of numbers.
source_location_hint: discourse on tawḥīd and the Battle of the Camel

excerpt: The Qur'an consists of a book inscribed between two covers; it speaks not with a tongue and cannot do without an interpreter.
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha and related Imam Ali compilations
source_authority: Primary teaching on interpretation and intellect
source_location_hint: discourse on the role of intellect in understanding revelation

excerpt: One purpose of revelation was to unearth for them the buried treasures of the intellects (dafāʾin al-ʿuqūl).
source_authority: Primary source on intellect and revelation
source_location_hint: opening sermon outlining divine purposes

excerpt: Eyes see Him not through sight's observation, but hearts see Him through the true principles and beliefs of faith.
source_authority: Teaching on spiritual vision and the heart
source_location_hint: discourse on divine perception and faith

excerpt: My Lord is not described in terms of dimension, nor motion, nor rest. He is subtle in subtlety but not described by subtlety. He is magnificent in magnificence but not described by magnificence.
source_location_hint: sermons on divine attributes and transcendence

excerpt: Remembrance of Allah is the most excellent form of worship.
source_work: Nahj al-Balagha and collections of Imam Ali's sayings
source_authority: Teaching on spiritual practice and devotion
source_location_hint: discourse on dhikr and the perfection of ikhlaṣ
